title: Homeroom Security: School Discipline in an Age of Fear By Aaron Kupchik New York University Press, 2010, 261 pages
abstract: Metal detectors, surveillance cameras, drug-sniffing dogs—these are not the images we associate with the typical American high school. However, as Aaron Kupchik explains in Homeroom Security: School Discipline in an Age of Fear, these are increasingly common characteristics of schools throughout the country. In the name of insuring order and safety, highly punitive discipline policies are increasingly being utilized in schools. Such measures are particularly pervasive in inner-city schools populated by low-income, Latino and African American students. However, as Kupchik shows, they are also showing up in middle-class, White suburban communities, and he uses his study of four American high schools to illustrate how this is occurring. Zero-tolerance policies, which typically require schools to mete out harsh penalties for even minor infractions, were enacted by states throughout the country in response to a number of highly publicized school shootings that occurred in the 1980s and 1990s. Combined with a growing police and security presence, such practices have become a common feature of the American high school. Kupchik uses a study of four high schools, two in the mid-Atlantic region and two in the Southwest, to draw attention to how punitive discipline practices are affecting schools. He selected schools with varying demographic profiles in order to ascertain how the racial and socioeconomic composition of schools influenced discipline practices. Through extensive observations, interviews and surveys, Kupchik shows that differences in the composition of the student body are insignificant.
